name: Mohannad Alotaibi
comment: el psy kongroo

name: Ba Quyen Chu
comment: Quyen

name: Tony Yong He
comment: Whoever designed the current curriculum doesn't seem to know what's going on in the outside world

name: Isitha Subasinghe
comment:
I found the programming language course to be quite interesting, but they cancelled it. 
If it wasn't for exchange, I would have had near zero CS knowledge outside of the algorithms & DS

name: Tuan Khanh Bui
program: Bachelor of Science - Major in Computing and Software Systems
comment: I also want Declarative Programming and some Master's subjects to have their own undergraduate version, though I do understand this is not an easy problem.

name: Anqi Wang
comment: Please make a separate subject for discrete mathematics.

name: Agastya Malik
comment: Not enough rigour I learnt more in my A levels

name: Caitlin Webb
comment:
Much the same issues mentioned in the letter. 

I found database systems and data modelling to be intro to sql with a side of here is how to model data. You have to know what ACID is but no training in actually implementing it. 

Internet technologies is wholly lifted from the textbook. The subject is literally read the textbook congrats how much can you remember. 

Algorithms and complexity and programming and software development were really good.

name: Aarya Vivekanandan
comment: Search up pihedron.

name: Austen McClernon
comment: The lack of rigorous computer systems subjects is extremely unfortunate. To graduate from a computer science degree never having built an operating system, compiler or database as part of the coursework is unheard of in any half-decent computer science program, which I sincerely hope unimelb cs aspires to be.

name: Chuan
comment: The CS curriculum feels too compressed. There are too few core CS electives, so students must spend credits on less relevant math or breadth subjects. OS and networks are merged into Computer Systems, giving less depth than separate courses, despite being key topics for jobs and interviews. Breadth subjects also rarely broaden learning; many students use them to reduce workload or boost grades.

name: Lily
comment: I was surprised that to complete the computer science major at unimelb, the only mathematical knowledge you need to acquire is linear algebra and calculus too. The course designer clearly does not understand the close relationship between computer science and advance mathematics. Please at least set discrete mathematics and propability as the requirements of computer science degree.

name: David Ye
comment: Agreed, strongly support

name: quang trần
comment:
I feel that the uni focus on Operating Systems and Low Level Programming is extremely lacking, whereas these are considered fundamental knowledge for a CS major in any other university around the world. 

Another critique I might add is IT project is focusing too much on Web / App development, whereas the actual CS field is so much more broad and used everywhere else (like Telecommunications, Bank technologies, Cyber Security, Low Level app,...)

name: HUGO
comment: As a Year 1 student, I feel disappointed with the course structure.

name: Dương Thiên
comment: Same thing for Data Science, so I need a letter like this for Data Science course as well. Machine Learning course was so outdated even internet could teach you better. All of my friends who studied CS in unimelb has said the same

name: Gary Tannert
comment: Agreed with this - alot of the courses being taught in Unimelb has a severe gap with what is expected in industry and does not set-up a good foundation for future learning.

name: Timothy Wong
comment: I support this letter. I am from a non-STEM background and have done a few intro to programming to courses as an undergraduate. The tutorial experience is by far the worst I've ever come across. In last week's, only 3 students attended including myself. Students clearly don't see the value in showing up for this degree, which is a shame.

name: J Lin
comment: There is a severe lack of low level subjects like compilers and taking programming language implementation off the curriculum is mind boggling. Many subjects feel extremely rushed (CCC, NLP), there is no time to understand a topic deeply. Time allocation across projects also feel poorly distributed and research based projects should have longer page limits.

name: J Sun
comment: While I do think a lot of these subjects are missing from the CS courses, some of the low level programming or systems do exist in some subjects in the EEE department. Lots of other universities have like combined EE and CS departments, where a lot of the contents actually overlap. Maybe unimelb can follow a similar path. Or at least make it possible for people from both departments who wants to learn more about each other's contents to be able to choose those subjects. Where as now a lot of the subjects have prerequisites where it is impossible for a CS student to study an EE subject, and vice versa. Or maybe even incorporate some of each other's subjects as core subjects.

name: John Ling
comment: The Melbourne Model is our home-grown, No Child Left Behind Act 😭. It's shameless how much the uni tries to push you into further study by not offering any industry placement programs yet refuses to teach the fundamental areas that could be explored in research. I built a compiler before coming to uni and I was looking forward to learning more about them but we don't even have this class at the Master's level anymore. This curriculum is such a joke and it's frustrating seeing how lobotomised it's become.

name: Chi Nguyen
comment: Had the worst experience with Database Systems in my first year, had to learn everything that actually matters for my technical interviews/internships on reddit :(

name: BT2580 h
comment: It is not frequent that the administrator switches the lecturer of the courses I enrolled.

name: Revant Srivastava
program: B-Des computing and software systems + UX design
comment: I chose UniMelb over other universities thinking that its prestige meant good courses. Wrong. The "Computing and Software Systems" major is, for lack of a better word, a scam. It is a mile wide but an inch deep, merely touching on theory across a plethora of topics while skipping foundational subjects that graduates actually need. By heavily favoring abstract theory, the curriculum forces students onto academic pathways rather than giving them the skills and choice to get into the industry.

name: Deen
comment: Missing fundamental computing content. Neither in depth nor in breadth...

name: Jeslyn Tsui
comment: Most classes are heavily theory-based, and involve a lot of self-learning when it comes to application. Hands-on experiences and connection to the industry are very limited.

name: jasper
comment:
Jasper Glover
First Year
